text: Two elliptical incisions are made over the umbilicus and the inverted inferior crease of the umbilicus, followed by a stab wound.

The surgeon makes an incision at the inferior crease using an #11 blade, then inserts a Veress needle into the incision, lifting the abdominal wall and positioning the needle perpendicular to the wall, pointing towards the anus. Two audible clicks are heard, followed by the irrigation test.

The surgeon performs the hanging drop test, followed by connecting the tubing of the insufflator to the incision site. The insufflation begins with a preset pressure of fifteen and a flow rate of one liter per minute, as the actual pressure gradually increases to match the preset pressure.

The surgeon completes pneumoperitoneum with an actual pressure of fourteen, then removes the Veress needle. The incision over the inferior crease of the umbilicus is enlarged, and a small mosquito forceps is used.

The surgeon dilates the vitreous into a channel track using the Scandinavian technique. The trocar is held like a pistol, with its head resting on a thin hard eminence, and is introduced into the abdominal cavity with a screwing motion.

The surgeon introduces the laparoscopic telescope, revealing extensive adhesions between the abdominal organs and the anterior abdominal wall. Due to limited space, the epigastric port is placed in the left hypochondrium.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform adhesiolysis, targeting the dense adhesions observed during diagnostic laparoscopy. The trocar is positioned in the left hypochondrium, and the left lobe of the liver is visibly attached to surrounding structures.

The surgeon observes the left lobe of the liver attached to the costal margins and ribcage, with the falciform ligament visible. The right lobe of the liver is also identified, but the gallbladder is obscured due to extensive adhesions. The surgeon begins adhesiolysis using a harmonic device to separate the adhesions.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis using a harmonic device, gradually separating the dense adhesions within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies adhesions caused by previous episodes of cholecystitis, noting the stomach and gallbladder are adhered to the omentum.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device to perform adhesiolysis, carefully separating the omentum adhered to the inferior margin of the liver. The silicone tip of the harmonic is kept oriented towards the gallbladder to prevent puncturing it.
The gallbladder is visible, completely impacted with a large stone measuring approximately 4.6 to 8 centimeters.

The surgeon has nearly completed separating adhesions from the fundus region of the gallbladder. In the Calot's triangle, additional adhesions are being separated using the harmonic device, while the left hand holds the omentum. The stomach is visible below the surgical field.

The surgeon separates the adhered duodenum from surrounding tissues. The fundus clasp is used to hold the gallbladder fundus and retract it towards the right shoulder, facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon uses an instrument to separate further adhesions between the stomach and surrounding tissues, carefully dissecting to free the stomach from any remaining attachments.

The surgeon identifies a band between the stomach and the cystic pedicle, pushing it down to reveal the common bile duct (CBD) and the duodenum below. The Hartmann's pouch is held with anteromedial traction, allowing the opening of the posterior peritoneum.

The surgeon opens the posterior peritoneum above the Rouviere's sulcus, separating one-third of the gallbladder from the liver. Additional adhesions in this area are carefully dissected to facilitate the posterior peritoneum dissection.

The surgeon mobilizes the stomach further downward and begins dissection of the posterior peritoneum by applying anteromedial traction. Due to the gallbladder being impacted with stones, the gallbladder is lifted to facilitate separation of the peritoneum between the gallbladder and liver.

The surgeon applies anteromedial traction to separate the posterior peritoneum above the roviate sulcus. Anterolateral traction is then used to separate the peritoneum on the interior side, isolating one-third of the gallbladder body. The dissection progresses downward, creating a visible posterior window.

The surgeon performs stripping of the peritoneum around the cystic pedicle, creating an anterior window. With both posterior and anterior windows established, the Calot's triangle is defined. Anterolateral traction is applied, revealing the inferior surface of the liver, which forms one boundary of the Calot's triangle in laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

The surgeon separates the peritoneum over the cystic pedicle, performing skeletonization of the cystic duct and cystic artery. Below, the common bile duct (CBD) is visible, indicating the anatomical level of the dissection.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to enlarge the anterior window by opening its jaws, following the skeletonization of the visceral peritoneum over the cystic pedicle with a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon creates a 2.2 cm window between the artery and the liver, with the inferior surface of the liver visible. The cystic duct appears slightly dilated and short. An extracorporeal Misrach knot is fed through the window, with the suture tail caught and tied outside using a sequence of hitches, winds, and locks.
The surgeon uses a knot pusher to tighten the extracorporeal knot near the common bile duct (CBD), ensuring a secure ligation. The knot is then cut, and a surgical clip is applied to the cystic duct.

A titanium clip is applied to the cystic duct near the gallbladder, ensuring both jaws of the clip are visible. Another clip is applied to the cystic artery, with both jaws visible. The harmonic scalpel is then used to cut, keeping the vibrating jaw under visual guidance.

The surgeon uses the teflon jaw to cut the knot securing the cystic duct, ensuring the artery is also cut. The cystic pedicle is visibly separated. The gallbladder is then separated from the liver by applying alternating anteromedial and anterolateral traction.
The surgeon cuts the peritoneal shoulders and separates the areolar tissue to facilitate the separation of the gallbladder. A large gallstone is visible, and an endobag is used to manage the stone.

The surgeon places the gallbladder, containing a single large stone, into a commercially available endo bag from Lotus. The endo bag is inserted into the surgical field, its mouth opened, and the gallbladder is introduced inside for secure removal.

The surgeon uses ovum forceps to crush the large gallbladder stone inside the endo bag, splitting it into two halves. The rectus muscle is stretched using large jaw artery forceps to facilitate the process.

The surgeon carefully removes the gallbladder through the epigastric port, maneuvering it right, left, up, and down. After extraction, the epigastric port is closed, with the rectus muscle slightly stretched, and the knot is tied externally.
The surgeon displays a large gallbladder stone, marking the completion of the procedure.
